title: Real Estate Deals in San Francisco CA: A Practical Investor's Guide for 2024
excerpt: San Francisco's real estate market is one of the most competitive in the country, but profitable deals still exist for investors who know where to look. This guide breaks down the best neighborhoods, deal types, and strategies for finding real estate deals in San Francisco CA.
author: AIWholesail Team
tags: real estate deals in San Francisco CA
metaDescription: Find profitable real estate deals in San Francisco CA. Explore top neighborhoods, market trends, flip and rental strategies, and tips for out-of-state investors.
metaKeywords: real estate deals in San Francisco CA, San Francisco investment properties, wholesale real estate San Francisco, fix and flip San Francisco, San Francisco rental properties, off-market deals San Francisco, San Francisco real estate investing

sections.content: San Francisco is one of the most talked-about real estate markets in the world. High prices, fierce competition, and strict regulations make it intimidating for many investors. But here's the truth: real estate deals in San Francisco CA still exist — you just need to know where to look and how to analyze them correctly. Whether you're a seasoned wholesaler, a first-time flipper, or a landlord chasing cash flow, this guide gives you a clear picture of what the San Francisco market looks like in 2024 and how to find your place in it. If you're newer to the game, start with our [Real Estate Investing for Beginners](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/real-estate-investing-for-beginners) guide before diving in.
San Francisco Real Estate Market Overview

San Francisco's real estate market is defined by scarcity and demand. The city covers just 49 square miles, and strict zoning laws limit new construction. As of mid-2024, the median home price in San Francisco sits around $1.3 million — down roughly 10 to 15 percent from the peak highs of 2021 and 2022, but still among the highest in the nation. Condos and smaller units have softened more than single-family homes, with median condo prices closer to $900,000 to $1.1 million depending on the neighborhood. For investors, this correction has created a window of opportunity that didn't exist two years ago. Motivated sellers, longer days on market, and price reductions are showing up more frequently — which means negotiating power is back on the table.
Best Neighborhoods for Real Estate Investors in San Francisco
Not every San Francisco neighborhood makes sense for every investment strategy. Here's a breakdown of the areas getting the most attention from active investors right now.
Bayview-Hunters Point

This southeast neighborhood has some of the lowest entry prices in the city, with single-family homes still available in the $700,000 to $900,000 range. Ongoing infrastructure investments and proximity to the waterfront have made it a long-term appreciation play. Wholesalers and flippers are most active here because the spread between distressed and retail prices is larger than in most SF neighborhoods.
Excelsior and Outer Mission

These working-class neighborhoods offer solid value for buy-and-hold investors. Multi-unit buildings — duplexes, triplexes, and small apartment buildings — trade here at prices that can still pencil for investors targeting long-term rental income. Rents in these areas average between $2,800 and $3,800 per month for a two-bedroom unit.
Ingleside and Oceanview

Often overlooked by coastal city buyers, Ingleside and Oceanview sit in the southern part of the city and offer some of the best price-per-square-foot value in San Francisco. Investors focused on light cosmetic flips have found consistent opportunities here, especially in older Victorian and Edwardian-era homes that need updating but have good structural bones.
Types of Real Estate Deals Available in San Francisco
Wholesale Deals

Wholesaling in San Francisco is challenging but not impossible. The key is finding deeply motivated sellers — probate estates, landlords tired of dealing with rent-controlled tenants, or out-of-state owners with properties in disrepair. Assignment fees in this market can range from $20,000 to $80,000 or more on a good deal, which makes it worth the effort. If you're new to this strategy, our [Wholesaling Real Estate for Beginners](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/wholesaling-real-estate-for-beginners) guide is a great starting point. To evaluate potential assignments quickly, use our free [Wholesale Deal Calculator](https://aiwholesail.com/tools/wholesale-deal-calculator).
Fix and Flip

Fix-and-flip deals in San Francisco require careful analysis because renovation costs are significantly higher than in most U.S. markets. Expect to pay $150 to $300 per square foot for quality renovation work. That said, a well-executed flip in a desirable neighborhood can still generate $150,000 to $400,000 in gross profit when purchased right. Always calculate your after-repair value before committing to a deal — use our free [ARV Calculator](https://aiwholesail.com/tools/arv-calculator) to run the numbers before you make an offer.
Rental Properties

San Francisco is one of the most rent-controlled cities in the country, which is a double-edged sword for landlords. Units built before 1979 are subject to strict rent control ordinances that limit annual increases and make evictions difficult. However, rents are among the highest in the nation — averaging $3,200 for a one-bedroom and $4,500 for a two-bedroom as of 2024. Investors who buy multi-unit properties with vacancies or in need of rehab can often bring rents closer to market rate. New construction and condos built after June 1979 are exempt from rent control, making them more landlord-friendly.
Key Market Trends Shaping San Francisco in 2024

items: Office-to-residential conversions: With downtown vacancy rates exceeding 30%, the city is actively incentivizing conversion projects — creating opportunities for commercial investors.
Tech sector stabilization: After waves of layoffs in 2022 and 2023, the tech industry is hiring again, which is pushing rental demand back up in neighborhoods like SoMa and the Mission.
Condo market softness: Oversupply in luxury condos has created buyer's market conditions in certain buildings, giving investors room to negotiate.

Rising insurance costs: Wildfire risk and general market conditions have driven homeowner's insurance premiums up 20 to 40 percent in parts of the Bay Area — a cost investors must factor into their analysis.
Longer days on market: The average home now sits on the market 30 to 45 days, compared to under 15 days during the 2021 peak — giving investors more time and leverage.
Probate and estate sales increasing: An aging population means more inherited properties are coming to market, often at below-market prices.

sections.content: The best time to buy San Francisco real estate was 20 years ago. The second best time is when everyone else is too scared to. Markets that feel uncomfortable are often where the real wealth gets built.
How to Find Off-Market Deals in San Francisco

The MLS in San Francisco is competitive, and on-market deals rarely leave room for investor margins. That's why finding off-market properties is essential in this city. Direct mail campaigns to absentee owners, probate court records, code violation lists, and networking with local real estate attorneys and CPAs who work with estate cases are among the most effective methods. For a full playbook on sourcing properties before they hit the market, check out our detailed guide on [How to Find Off-Market Real Estate Deals](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/off-market-real-estate-deals). Driving for dollars in neighborhoods like Bayview and Excelsior can also surface properties that never make it online. Look for signs of deferred maintenance — overgrown yards, boarded windows, or peeling paint — and track them systematically.

Pro Tip: In San Francisco, many profitable deals come from landlords exhausted by rent control battles or difficult tenants. Search county records for properties with multiple code enforcement complaints — these owners are often highly motivated to sell quickly and at a discount.
Practical Tips for Out-of-State Investors

San Francisco is one of the most complex markets for out-of-state investors to navigate. The combination of rent control, transfer tax, seismic retrofit requirements, and tenant protection laws means you need local expertise in your corner. Here are the most important things to know before investing from a distance.

items: Hire a local property manager before you buy: San Francisco's landlord-tenant laws are among the most tenant-friendly in the country. A qualified local property manager is not optional — it's essential.

Understand the Transfer Tax: San Francisco charges a transfer tax between 0.5% and 3% depending on the sale price. On a $1 million purchase, that's up to $30,000 in closing costs you need to account for.

Budget for seismic retrofitting: Older soft-story buildings (those with garages or open ground floors) may be required to complete seismic upgrades. These can cost $50,000 to $150,000 and must be factored into your acquisition price.

Use technology to analyze deals remotely: Platforms like AIWholesail let you run deal analysis, find motivated sellers, and evaluate neighborhoods without being on the ground every week. Explore the [San Francisco CA market data](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/san-francisco-ca) to start building your local knowledge.
Visit before you close: No matter how good the numbers look online, always walk the property and the neighborhood in person before closing on a deal in this market.

Network with local investors: Join Bay Area Real Estate Investor Association (BARIA) meetups or BiggerPockets forums specific to the SF market. Local investors will share knowledge you can't find anywhere else.

sections.content: Analyzing San Francisco Deals: Run the Numbers First

No matter how exciting a property looks, the numbers have to make sense. San Francisco's high purchase prices mean thin margins are easy to miss if you're not rigorous. For rentals, calculate your gross rent multiplier, cap rate, and cash-on-cash return before making any offer. For flips, work backward from your conservative ARV estimate and subtract all holding, renovation, and selling costs. Our guide on [How to Analyze Real Estate Deals Like a Pro](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/how-to-analyze-real-estate-deals) walks you through the exact framework experienced investors use to evaluate any deal in any market.

Pro Tip: In San Francisco, always use a conservative ARV when running flip projections. Overestimating your after-repair value by even 5% in a $1.4M property means a $70,000 error that can wipe out your entire profit margin.
